message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Justification</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Stimulation of early innate anti-viral responses during the early phase of SARS-COV-2 infection oxygen may improve evolution of illness and late pulmonary complications. This may be possible using a TLR agonist such as a probiotic bacterium possessing desirable immunomodulatory properties.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We performed a non-contact, open-label, prospective randomized clinical trial comparing intranasally applied
                    <jats:italic>Lactococcus lactis W136</jats:italic>
                    with saline irrigation alone in patients within 96 hours of diagnosis of SARS-COV-2 infection not requiring supplemental oxygen.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Twenty-three of a planned forty participants aged 18-59 without comorbidities were recruited. Irrigation with intranasal
                    <jats:italic>L lactis W136</jats:italic>
                    twice-daily for fourteen days of was associated with a nasal response characterised by increase in the symptom of Facial and Throat pain/discomfort, and with a lesser severity in symptoms of i) Fatigue ii) Olfactory dysfunction and iii) Breathlessness. Safety and tolerability were good, with no acute infections or severe deteriorations.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Facial and throat pain may correspond to postulated mechanism of action corresponding to activation of innate defences with antiviral effects and may explain the potentially protective effects seen. Intranasal
                    <jats:italic>L lactis W136</jats:italic>
                    irrigations may thus represent a potentially inexpensive, safe, and easily scalable non-antigen based therapeutic for the continuing global SARS-COV-2 pandemic.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data availability statement</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Data is available on request from the senior author, Dr Desrosiers:
                    <jats:italic>martin-yvon</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>desrosiers</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>med@ssss</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>gouv</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>qc</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>ca</jats:italic>
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Funding</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This work was supported with internal funds from the Desrosiers laboratory at the Centre de Recherche du Centre Hospitaller de l’Université de Montreal (CRCHUM).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Competing Interests</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Dr Desrosiers holds equity in Probionase Therapies inc., Which commercialises
                    <jats:italic>Lactococcus lactis W136</jats:italic>
                    for chronic rhinosinusitis.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: INTRANASAL APPLICATION OF LACTOCOCCUS LACTIS W 136 BACTERIA EARLY IN SARS-Cov-2 INFECTION MAY HAVE A BENEFICIAL IMMUNOMODULATORY EFFECT: A PROOF-OF-CONCEPT STUDY
